lines.text: We're {{ELAPSED}} in. The clock reads {{CLOCK}}. And folks, I gotta tell you, the calls are starting to come in, and they are not the calls I was hoping for tonight.
I've got whiskey. I've got time. And I've got a spreadsheet in front of me that Tim just slid over that says the East Side is already tracking twelve percent above last year at this hour.
Twelve percent. We're two hours in. That's not noise. That's a trend.
You know what today was? Before the Purge started? It was March 24th. Ring any bells for the long-time listeners?
Caller: Denis from Schenks Corners — go ahead, Denis.

Caller: Denis from Schenks Corners — 'Quinn, I'm locked in my basement, my wife's upstairs with the TV up loud so nobody knows we're here, and I just wanted to hear another human voice.' — Denis, that's why we're here. You're not alone.
Denis, is your barricade solid? Be honest with me.

Caller: Denis from Schenks Corners — 'Good. Real good. Panels went in last Tuesday. Cost me eight grand, but I figure it's cheaper than the alternative.' — That's the arithmetic right there, folks. That's the Purge in a nutshell.
Denis, you'll make it through. Stay off the phone, keep the TV loud, and for God's sake don't answer the door.
Caller: Denis from Schenks Corners — 'I wouldn't dream of it, Quinn. Not on my worst day.' — Good man. See you on the other side, Denis.
You know what kills me? And I mean this in the most literal sense possible tonight — every year I sit here at {{CLOCK}} thinking about four years ago.
I'm not going to say the name. I'm not going to do that to Tim's signal. But four years ago, right around this time, State Street was a bloodbath.

March 24th, 2165. Twenty-three people in forty minutes. A coordinated action. And I remember sitting in this chair at this hour, and the kill count wasn't even updated yet, and I was already drinking.
I'm telling you this because I'm already drinking. And I want you to know that I *remember* why.
You pour the first one thinking you're just calming the nerves. By the third one, you're being honest.
Caller: Margaret from Midvale — 'Quinn, I'm sorry to bother you, but I heard the East Side was hot. My sister lives on Atwood.' — Margaret, is she sheltering in place?

Caller: Margaret from Midvale — 'Yes, God yes, she's locked down with her family. But I keep thinking about 2165, and I'm wondering if there's a pattern.' — Margaret, there's no pattern. There's no way to predict this. The only thing you can do is what your sister is already doing.
Caller: Margaret from Midvale — 'I just needed to hear you say it.' — I'm saying it. She's going to be fine. You're going to be fine.
Caller: Margaret from Midvale — 'Thank you, Quinn. I'm going to go sit with my husband now.' — Do that. Stay close to the people in your house.
That's the night I used to have, folks. Before the scandal. Before I became the guy who can't show his face downtown.
I was a senator. I had a wife. I had a house in Maple Bluff that's probably worth half what it used to be, because everybody knows where a disgraced politician's house is.
And one Purge Night — not the night I'm thinking of, a different one — I made some choices I'm not proud of.

I'm not going to detail them. That's not what tonight's about. But I will say this: the Purge had nothing to do with those choices. I made them because I was a coward and a fool on a day that started with the sun.
Pours whiskey audibly.
That's number four. I'm keeping count now because Margaret's anxiety just handed me my own.
Caller: Robert from North Side — 'Quinn, I've got a question about something I heard in the news block earlier.' — Robert, go ahead. News blocks are why we're here.

Caller: Robert from North Side — 'They said the government is considering making the Purge twelve hours in some cities and fourteen in others. Did I hear that right?' — Robert, you did not hear that. That's not something I can talk about on an unlicensed frequency.
Caller: Robert from North Side — 'But is it true?' — What's true is that you're safe where you are, and that's what matters tonight.
Caller: Robert from North Side — 'I guess that's fair. Stay safe, Quinn.' — You too, Robert.
Four years ago, when 2165 came around, I remember thinking about the future. I remember thinking, 'This will get better. This will change. People will wake up.'
They didn't wake up. They got smart. And smart is worse.
The people who used to be violent out of rage are now violent out of calculation. The Purge has been systematized. It's been industrialized. You can *see* it in the data.
Holden showed me those numbers. Twelve percent up. That's not random. That's preparation.

Caller: Lisa from the East Side — 'Quinn, I'm in an apartment building with seventeen other units and I just heard glass break two floors below me.' — Lisa, lock your door. Lock it now. Are you alone?

Caller: Lisa from the East Side — 'I'm here with my daughter. She's asleep. Should I wake her up?' — No. No, you shouldn't. Keep her asleep. Let her stay in a world where this night is just a bad dream.
Caller: Lisa from the East Side — 'I can hear shouting now.' — Lisa, if anyone comes to your door, you don't answer it. Not for any reason. Are you in a unit with a second exit?
Caller: Lisa from the East Side — 'There's a fire escape.' — Know where it is. Keep that window clear. And Lisa? You're going to be fine. Your daughter is going to wake up tomorrow.
Caller: Lisa from the East Side — 'How do you know that, Quinn?' — Because I've sat here every year for eight years, and the vast majority of people in locked rooms live through it.

Caller: Lisa from the East Side — 'What about the minority?' — We don't talk about the minority on this show, Lisa. We talk about the majority. We tell the people hiding that they made the right choice.
Lisa hung up. I don't know if she'll call back. Some people just need to hear it once.
And some people need to hear it all night.

The thing about 2165 is that nobody saw it coming. We thought the East Side was stable. I had a briefing memo from the police commissioner — this was before I was run out of office — that said East Side was the safest neighborhood in Madison.
Safe. In Madison. During the Purge. In 2165.
The memo was dated March 23rd. The Purge was March 24th.
Four years is a long time to hold onto something like that.
I think that's why I remember the date. I think that's why every time March rolls around, I start thinking about what I didn't know.
Caller: Vincent from Sun Prairie — 'Quinn, this is my first Purge listening to your station. I'm terrified.' — Vincent, welcome. I'm glad you found us. What's your situation?

Caller: Vincent from Sun Prairie — 'I'm in a house with my wife and our two kids. We've got security system, reinforced doors, the whole deal. But I'm from out of state and I don't understand why this is happening.' — Vincent, that's a question that runs deeper than tonight. But tonight, you're locked in. You're with people you love. That's enough.
Caller: Vincent from Sun Prairie — 'It doesn't feel like enough.' — I know. It never does. But it is.
Caller: Vincent from Sun Prairie — 'How long have you been doing this?' — Eight years. Eight Purge Nights. Same frequency, different faces in the audience.
Caller: Vincent from Sun Prairie — 'Have you ever had a night where nobody died?' — No, Vincent. I haven't.
Caller: Vincent from Sun Prairie — 'Is it always this bad?' — It's always something. Sometimes it's worse.
Vincent's probably going to sit with that answer for a while. That's fair. It's the answer I've got.
Pours again. Fifth one.
You know what I miss? I miss not knowing the kill count. I miss being a person who could say, 'The Purge happened and I didn't pay attention to the numbers.'
But that person is gone. That was a person who believed in things. That was a person who thought that the system could be fixed from the inside.
He was an idiot.
The system doesn't get fixed. It gets worse. And every year it gets worse in new ways.
2165 was coordinated. 2166 was weapons. 2167 was weather-suppressed. 2168 was something worse — 2168 was professional.
I've talked to people. I've listened to things. I know people who know things. And what I know is that the Purge isn't an accident anymore. It's not a consequence of removing law. It's a feature.
Caller: Patricia from Williamson Street — 'Quinn, we've got the barricade up. We've got people on watch. We're doing okay.' — Patricia, that's good. That's what I want to hear.
Caller: Patricia from Williamson Street — 'I'm calling because I remember the Williamson Street stand last year. Were you here for that?' — I was, Patricia. That was a night I remember well.
Caller: Patricia from Williamson Street — 'We held. Didn't lose anybody in the perimeter.' — You did. You held the line. I was there — well, not there, but I heard it. I remember it.

Caller: Patricia from Williamson Street — 'We're doing it again tonight. Neighborhood watch is armed. We're taking shifts. I wanted you to know that your words last year mattered. They helped us organize.' — Patricia, that's good. That's why I'm here.
Caller: Patricia from Williamson Street — 'If we make it through, I'm buying the station a case of whiskey.' — Patricia, you make it through and we'll drink to that on the other side of the Purge.
Patricia's got her community. That's the thing I've noticed over eight years — the neighborhoods that survive are the ones that organize. The ones that talk to each other before the Purge comes.
And the thing about 2165 is that nobody organized. Nobody talked. Nobody believed it could happen on their street.
It happened anyway.

I'm not trying to bring anyone down here. I'm not trying to make anyone more scared than they already are. But I'm a man who believes in precedent, and precedent says that if you're twelve percent hot at {{CLOCK}}, you're going to stay hot.
That's just mathematics. That's just the way the night flows.

Caller: James from Capitol Square — 'Quinn, I work in the Capitol. I'm not calling from there, I want to be clear. But I know the building's got a capacity for this. I know the emergency protocols.' — James, I appreciate the call, but I can't engage with that right now.
Caller: James from Capitol Square — 'I'm just saying the building is prepared. More prepared than you'd think.' — I'm sure it is. Buildings like that always are.
Caller: James from Capitol Square — 'But what about the rest of us?' — That's the question, James. That's always the question.
James, I'm going to let you go. Think about the people in your house. Think about how you can protect them.
The building's prepared. Of course the building's prepared. The Capitol's always prepared. That's the deal we made, back when the Purge was being sold as something that would reduce crime.
You remember that pitch? 'One night of legal violence will reduce the rest of the year.' Release the pressure valve. Let people get it out of their system.
It didn't work that way. It never works that way. What it did was train people. It systematized. It made killers into professionals.
And the people who proposed it? They're protected. They live in buildings. They have guards.
I used to be that person. I used to have that protection. Not the building, but the assumption that I was safe because I was important.
Then I wasn't important anymore. And suddenly I was very aware of how little protection importance gives you.

Caller: Steve from Vilas Park — 'Quinn, I just want to say thank you. I've been listening for three years and I haven't missed a Purge.' — Steve, that's dedication. That's a person who's committed to staying conscious.
Caller: Steve from Vilas Park — 'My family thinks I'm crazy. They want to sleep. But I can't sleep on Purge Night. I never could.' — Steve, you're not crazy. You're aware.

Caller: Steve from Vilas Park — 'Is it always like this? Does every year feel like it's going to be worse?' — Yes. And that's the answer I keep coming back to. Every year feels worse because every year we get better at it.
Caller: Steve from Vilas Park — 'Better at what?' — At the Purge. At killing. At systematizing violence.
Caller: Steve from Vilas Park — 'I don't know if I can keep doing this.' — You can. You're three years in. Stay the course.
Steve hung up. And I don't know what he'll do with that advice, but I had to give it.
We're {{ELAPSED}} in. Two hours. We've got ten hours left. And I've got five whiskeys in me and a calendar that keeps pointing back to four years ago.
I'm going to have another drink. I'm going to take more calls. And I'm going to keep telling people that they're going to be fine, because that's what this station does.
We lie together. We survive together.
That's the Purge. That's the only thing that makes it bearable.

tickerItems.text: Madison-wide cumulative: 38 kills at {{CLOCK}} (19% above 2168 pace at this hour). East Side: 8 confirmed. Capitol Square: 2.
East Side reporting elevated activity. Atwood Ave, Schenks Corners showing above-average engagement. Recommend caution for outlying residents.
Williamson Street neighborhood watch maintaining active perimeter at Yahara River bridge. No breaches reported. Status: contained.
Text-in: 'Quinn, my sister in the Capitol works nights. Should I be worried?' — No. The building's secure. She's fine. Stay focused on your own shelter.
